name: Aanaar
classification_notes: Aanaar is a humanist sans with a single-storey 'a' option and open letterforms, originally designed for children's literacy with clear letter differentiation and generous x-height typical of humanist sans design.
summary: A humanist sans originally designed for Sápmi minority-language children's textbooks, with deliberate letterform distinctions to aid early readers.
description: Aanaar grew from a self-initiated project to support literacy in Sápmi minority languages, addressing specific legibility problems common in children's typefaces. A spur on the top right of the single-storey 'a', a raised joint and slight narrowing all prevent confusion with 'o' and 'g', while a tall x-height and gentle rhythm aid reading flow. The re-released library edition adds two new weights and two-storey alternates via stylistic sets, making the family usable beyond the classroom.
foundry_blurb: Letterjuice describe Aanaar as a re-thought edition of a typeface originally created for Sápmi minority-language children's textbooks, now expanded with a larger character set, two additional weights and stylistic sets offering two-storey 'a' and 'g' forms for more advanced readers and broader editorial use.

name: Arlette
classification_notes: A sans-serif that deliberately departs from neutrality, drawing on Roger Excoffon's Choc for its fast stroke energy and expressive curves, placing it firmly in the expressive or experimental sans-serif territory rather than any neutral grotesque lineage.
summary: An experimental sans-serif drawing on Roger Excoffon's Choc for its stroke energy, extended with rich swash sets and full Thai coverage across eight weights.
description: Built as a deliberate reaction against Helvetica-like homogeneity, Arlette takes its formal cues from the fast, energetic curves of Excoffon's Choc and applies them across a Latin family of eight weights with italics, all featuring an unusually extensive set of swashes extended to accented and special glyphs. The Thai script was developed with Fiona Ross as consultant, adapting the same expressive stroke movement and adding alternate characters drawn from handwritten palm-leaf forms. Awards from Print mag, Hii Type and Granshan's Thai category reflect a genuinely ambitious multilingual undertaking.
foundry_blurb: Letterjuice describe Arlette as experimental yet functional, built on two main concepts: Roger Excoffon's work, particularly the fast stroke movement of Choc's lowercase c, and a reaction to the proliferation of near-identical sans-serifs. The typeface includes an extensive swash set as a tribute to Excoffon, applied beyond italics into the romans and extended to cover accented glyphs across all supported languages. Thai was developed with consultant Fiona Ross, adapting Arlette's expressive curves naturally to that script and adding alternates inspired by handwritten palm-leaf letterforms.

name: Baldufa
classification_notes: Baldufa is a serif with rounded serifs, irregular vertical stems and ink traps drawn from old printing idiosyncrasies; its deliberately imperfect, humanist character places it outside clean transitional or old-style categories.
summary: A multi-script serif spanning Latin, Arabic, Cyrillic and Greek, distinguished by rounded serifs, ink traps and a deliberately imperfect character drawn from old printing methods.
description: Rounded serifs, irregular vertical stems and extremely thin junctions give Baldufa a warmth and quirkiness that sets it apart from conventional text serifs. The italic drops the serifs entirely for greater expressivity, while Arabic Naskh softens traditional calligraphic contrast and adds rounded terminals to match the Latin's overall texture. Multiple Granshan, TDC and Morisawa awards recognise the quality of the multi-script design, which was built specifically for cultural catalogues and publications requiring harmonious cross-script setting.
foundry_blurb: Letterjuice describe Baldufa as a charming, strong-personality typeface designed for text comfort, with curves and details that bring elegance alongside a self-conscious appreciation for quirkiness and human imperfection. The family spans Latin, Arabic, Greek and Cyrillic, designed to work harmoniously together for multi-script cultural publications, with the Latin informed by old printing distortions, Greek pushed to maximum dynamic expressivity, and Arabic stepping away from traditional calligraphic style while retaining key structural features.

name: Quars
classification_notes: Quars draws from Scotch Roman contrast and the sharpness of contemporary Dutch designers, placing it firmly in the transitional serif tradition with a contemporary editorial edge.
summary: A text and display serif drawing on Scotch Roman contrast and Dutch sharpness, designed primarily for magazine and editorial use.
description: Quars offers five styles spanning regular, italic, bold, black and a dedicated display cut, covering both running text and titling needs. Its italic is set at just 1 degree of slant, a deliberate choice that preserves uppercase letterforms from the distortion common in more steeply inclined italics. The glyph set includes discretionary ligatures crafted for titling, along with ornaments derived from anonymous metal types found in an old printing workshop near Barcelona.
foundry_blurb: Letterjuice describe Quars as a text and display typeface family designed to work on magazines, though also suitable for books and other editorial material. They cite influences ranging from the contrast of Scotch Romans to the sharpness of contemporary Dutch designers, and highlight its crystal-clear structure, sharp contemporary features and generous glyph set including specially crafted ligatures and ornaments sourced from metal type found in a coastal Catalan printing workshop.

name: Isard
classification_notes: A multipurpose sans serif with squarish curves, large x-height and warm proportions pointing to a humanist classification, designed for both text and display use across Latin and Hebrew scripts.
summary: A bilingual sans serif in Latin and Hebrew covering seven weights with italics, built around a large x-height and squarish curves for use across information design, branding and editorial contexts.
description: Named after the mountain goat of the Catalan Pyrenees, it carries warmth and approachability through its squarish curves and generous x-height without sacrificing the legibility needed for complex typesetting. The family is sold in separate Hebrew, Latin and combined Hebrew-Latin packs, with fourteen styles each, allowing buyers to licence only what they need. Its range of weights from Thin to Black makes it functional across packaging, advertising and magazine layouts as well as interface and information design.
foundry_blurb: Letterjuice describe Isard as a very agile, multipurpose sans serif named after the Catalan Pyrenean mountain goat. They position it as a sturdy workhorse with down-to-earth elegance, built for information design and branding alike. Contemporary squarish curves and a large x-height prioritise legibility, while warmth and friendliness make it suitable for advertising, packaging and magazine work. The superfamily covers Latin and Hebrew scripts across seven weights from Thin to Black, each with a matching italic.

name: Nawin
classification_notes: Nawin is explicitly described as an informal typeface inspired by handwriting, with letter shapes emulating pen movement to express spontaneity. It sits firmly in the script/informal category despite its multi-script breadth and text-reading intent.
summary: A handwriting-inspired informal family spanning Latin, Arabic, Cyrillic and Greek, designed with children's reading in mind and a wide range of contextual alternates to emulate the natural variability of written letterforms.
description: Built around the spontaneous movement of the pen, Nawin uses contextual alternates to introduce subtle variation between repeated letterforms, counteracting the mechanical regularity of digital type. In Arabic, vertical proportion shifts between letters such as Alef and initial Lam create rhythm in running text, while Latin, Greek and Cyrillic achieve similar liveliness through alternate character sets including diacritic variants. The result balances quirk and legibility across five weights, sold separately by script or in combined packs. The family won an ED Award in 2023.
foundry_blurb: Letterjuice describe Nawin as an informal typeface inspired by handwriting, created to be attractive and ownable for children while maintaining strong letter recognition for reading. By emulating pen movement, the design achieves spontaneous letter shapes, supported by extensive contextual alternates that replicate the natural variety of handwritten forms. Arabic ligatures, Arabic contextual alternates for repeated similar letterforms, and a Paneuropean pack covering Latin, Arabic, Cyrillic and Greek round out the family's offerings.

name: Athenaia
classification_notes: Athenaia is a Greek-script serif with shifting personalities across weights: the Regular is a moderate-contrast text face, the Thin adopts a classical slab-serif feel, and the Black moves toward high-contrast display. The dominant design logic sits closest to transitional serif territory.
summary: A Greek-only serif family of five weights whose design personality shifts markedly from the classical slab-influenced Thin through to a high-contrast display Black.
description: Athenaia is built around the idea that a typeface's character can shift substantially across its weight range rather than simply scaling up. The Regular offers moderate contrast suited to long-form editorial text, the Thin draws on 1930s–40s slab serif conventions with a contemporary inflection, and the Black leans into high contrast for titling, packaging and branding. The family supports Greek script only, with OpenType features including cap spacing, case-sensitive forms, arrows and ornamental hands.
foundry_blurb: Letterjuice describe Athenaia as an experimental yet functional Greek typeface that explores the boundaries between type styles within the same family. The five-weight family offers three very distinctive personalities: the Regular is optimised for editorial text legibility, the Thin references classical slab serifs of the 1930s and 40s with a contemporary twist, and the Black is a display style intended for titling, packaging and branding.
